title: Compliance officers grapple with AI risks as upper management pushes adoption

I wanted to review it from a compliance angle.

title: Victory! 702 has Expired!
summary: Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act lets US intelligence agencies collect communications from foreigners abroad without a warrant, and routinely sweeps in Americans’ emails, messages, and calls in the process. The authority for this program is set to expire Friday, June 12th, 2026, at midnight. As we wrote earlier this week, Congress has been kicking the ball down the road for months now—temporarily postponing the expiration of the mass surveillance authority Section 702 of FISA in hopes that some consensus on a longer reauthorization could be reached. title: Over 400 Arch Linux AUR Packages Hijacked to Deploy Infostealer and eBPF Rootkit
summary: Attackers took over more than 400 packages in the Arch User Repository (AUR) this week and rewrote their build scripts to install a credential stealer on any machine that built them. The malware is a Rust binary built to harvest developer secrets. When it lands with root, it can also load an eBPF rootkit to hide itself. It looks less like a neighborhood and more like a geological formation — a dense, irregular mass of concrete and rusted metal that grew not upward but inward, filling every possible gap until nothing remained but structure. title: How to use NIST and ISO frameworks to govern AI agents
summary: Security leaders no longer need convincing that AI agents introduce risk. What’s missing is how to govern them once they move into production and begin operating autonomously across enterprise environments. AI agents already read sensitive documents, invoke internal APIs, trigger workflows, and make decisions that still require human judgment. From a security perspective, the most important shift is not their intelligence, but their behavior and intent, since they carry delegated authority, operate autonomously, and often … More → The post How to use NIST and ISO frameworks to gove

title: ZeroFox releases AI Analytics to bring answers directly to security teams
summary: ZeroFox launched ZeroFox AI Analytics, a new platform capability that gives security teams real-time visibility into the signals, patterns, and trends shaping their external threat landscape. ZeroFox AI Analytics gives security teams the ability to move beyond static reports and query their data in real time. Rather than waiting on manual exports or scheduled summaries, teams can immediately surface what is driving a spike in alerts, identify emerging patterns across their threat landscape, and understand … More → The post ZeroFox releases AI Analytics to bring answers directly to

title: The assembly line behind 1.5 million malicious domains
summary: Attackers registered roughly 1.5 million malicious domains during the first five months of 2026. The registration patterns resemble industrial output. Most of the domains were created by attackers, put to use within weeks, and concentrated among a small set of registrars, top-level domains, and hosting providers. New research examined more than 1.5 million unique domains flagged on VirusTotal between January and May 2026. Each domain was flagged by at least five independent VirusTotal scanning engines … More → The post The assembly line behind 1.5 million malicious domains appeared

title: AI sovereignty makes data centers strategic targets for cyber operations
summary: Data centers built for frontier AI draw hundreds of megawatts of electricity and large volumes of cooling water from fixed locations with known addresses. Each one concentrates tens of thousands of graphics processors, liquid cooling systems, and high-density power equipment inside a single building. This physical footprint turns a nation’s AI capability into something an adversary can locate, measure, and degrade. Mechanisms determining AI sovereignty at the micro, meso, and macro levels AI sovereignty is … More → The post AI sovereignty makes data centers strategic targets

title: Product showcase: Avast One turns scam screenshots into actionable security advice
summary: Avast One Free combines privacy, security, identity monitoring, and performance tools in a single platform. The app is available for Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS.
